output_text:
Critique of the paragraph, focused on substantive issues rather than style.

**The Halabi citation is the highest-priority concern.** I cannot verify that Halabi et al. published a verbalized-confidence chest-radiograph paper in *Radiology: Artificial Intelligence* in 2021. Halabi is best known as a co-organizer of the 2019 RSNA Intracranial Hemorrhage challenge, which is a head-CT dataset — exactly the modality you claim to be extending *into*. The verbalized-confidence line of work is more commonly traced to Lin, Hilton & Evans (2022, "Teaching models to express their uncertainty in words") and follow-ups by Tian et al. (2023), Xiong et al. (2024), etc., none of which are in *Radiology: AI*. Two possibilities: (a) the citation is genuine and I'm unaware of it — in which case the section needs DOI/volume/page details so a reviewer can find it; (b) the citation is wrong or invented — in which case the entire framing ("building on the chest-radiograph foundation laid by Halabi et al.", "first volumetric extension") collapses. **Verify this citation before submission.** If it doesn't exist, you cannot keep the "first" framing without rewriting around the actual prior art.

**Internal inconsistency on confidence vocabulary.** Para 2 introduces a three-phrase vocabulary ("low", "moderate", "high"). Para 3 says the model emits "one of five confidence phrases". Either Halabi used three and you extended to five (state this and justify), or this is a slip.

**Coverage is missing from the selective-prediction claim.** "94.1% sensitivity and 91.6% specificity at the 'high' threshold, exceeding the average radiologist working point" is meaningless without the coverage fraction. If only 12% of cases receive "high", you have not exceeded radiologists — you have deferred 88% of them. Report coverage alongside sens/spec, and compare to radiologists on the same selected subset.

**The "radiologist working point" comparison needs scoping.** How many radiologists, what experience level, were they reading under the same conditions (priors, clinical context, time pressure)? A single sentence cannot bear this much weight. Either cite the reader study or downgrade the claim.

**ECE methodology is invisible.** You report ECE on a discrete five-bin verbal output. How are phrases mapped to scalar probabilities for the ECE calculation? Empirical bin frequencies? Learned mapping? The temperature-scaled baseline operates on continuous logits, so the comparison isn't apples-to-apples unless you explain the binning protocol for both.

**Distribution shift is narrower than the framing suggests.** "Different scanner manufacturer" is one axis. Real deployment shift includes patient demographics, acquisition protocol, slice thickness, reconstruction kernel, and prevalence. Either broaden the eval or narrow the claim from "realistic distribution shift" to "scanner-manufacturer shift".

**Motivation/results mismatch.** Para 1 motivates the work against MC dropout and deep ensembles. Para 4 only benchmarks against temperature scaling. Add at least one Bayesian/ensemble baseline or drop them from the motivation.

**Unspecified backbone.** "A CLIP-style encoder pretrained on radiology report pairs" — name it (CheXzero, BiomedCLIP, MedCLIP, or your own pretraining) and cite. Reviewers will ask.

**"First" claims are reviewer bait.** Even if Halabi et al. is real, "first verbalized-confidence model on volumetric neuroimaging" is the kind of statement that one obscure arXiv preprint can refute. Soften to "to our knowledge, the first" and run a focused literature sweep (arXiv 2023–2025, MIDL, MICCAI uncertainty workshops) before locking it in.

**Minor:** "Bayesian approximations such as MC dropout … and deep ensembles" — deep ensembles are not generally considered a Bayesian approximation; many authors specifically contrast them with Bayesian methods. Rephrase as "approximate-Bayesian and ensemble methods".

Uncertainty disclosure: My claim that the Halabi 2021 *Radiology: AI* citation does not exist is based on my own recall of the verbalized-confidence and radiology-AI literatures and could be wrong — please verify directly against the journal's archive or a database search rather than taking my word for it. The attribution of verbalized confidence to Lin et al. 2022 is the conventional origin in the LLM-calibration literature but other groups have parallel claims.
